raw_case:
Haemangioma of the breast: The added value of contrast enhanced ultra-sound (CEUS) in the vascularity assessment of a rare mesenchymal lesion
Haemangiomas have been described in many anatomic locations, but occur quite rarely in the breast; although unfrequent, they are the most common benign vascular tumors of the breast. They are comprised in the cathegory of mesenchymal lesions, a spectrum of lesions that arise in the stroma of the breast as defined by WHO.Here we present the case of a breast haemangioma in a 40-years-old woman, with history of a palpable lump in the left breast slowly increasing in size over time. The lesion first underwent diagnostic biopsy in 2018; after a 5-years follow-up with conventional ultrasound, it was studied in 2023 by our group using Contrast Enhanced Ultra-Sound (CEUS) in order to evaluate its vascularization. Basing on our experience, we conclude that in carefully selected cases of histologically proven breast haemangioma, a conservative management with follow-up imaging is safe; CEUS represents a noninvasive method for studying haemangiomas, superior than traditional US thanks to its ability to provide information about vascularity. In those cases with atypical imaging or clinical pathological suspicious characteristics, a complete surgical resection of the vascular tumor is mandatory–in order to exclude the possibility of an underlying angiosarcoma, especially when the lesion measures more than 2 cm.

Introduction

There are 17 lesions encompassed in the WHO histological classification of mesenchymal tumors of the breast [1]; benign vascular tumors of the breast include haemangioma, lymphangioma and angiolipoma.Haemangioma accounts for only 0.4% of all breast tumors [[2], [3], [4]]. A retrospective review of 10,000 breast biopsies performed within a ten-year period by Glazebrook et al. in 2005 [5] found only 18 cases of benign vascular breast tumors; fifteen of these cases were haemangiomas, while 3 were angiolipomas.Haemangiomas of the breast are usually less than 2 cm in diameter [1]. They present in the sixth decade of life, typically with a female predilection. Haemangiomas are sub-categorized as “capillary” or “cavernous” depending on the size of the vascular channels, with cavernous being more common in the breast [16]. Histologically, the diagnosis is based on the presence of dilated blood-filled channels lined by endothelial cells without signs of atypia [6,7]. Benign hemangiomas tend to be more superficial than angiosarcomas, which have more glandular location.Breast haemangiomas can be found incidentally on screening mammography. On clinical examination, a haemangioma located in the subdermal or subcutaneous breast tissue may be detected as a palpable mass [8]. The mammographic appearance of a breast haemangioma is generally of a small well-defined mass, with lobular shape and circumscribed margins, with or without calcifications [1,9]. On ultrasound (US), breast haemangiomas may appear as hypoechoic, hyperechoic or isoechoic masses, usually with parallel orientation, or either remain occult [9,10]. They may be difficult to differentiate from complex cysts or fibroadenomas on US. Hypovascularity at Color Doppler module with a single vascular pole is more often associated with benign lesions; hypervascularity with multiple peripheral poles is more common in malignant lesions.At Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), breast haemangiomas appear as lobulated masses usually isointense with muscle in T1w images and hyperintense on T2w images [9]. There may be hypointense areas in the lesion because of calcifications, phleboliths and fibrous tissue septa in T2w images. Haemangiomas may demonstrate delayed heterogeneous enhancement, or early diffuse fast enhancement followed by a plateau or a wash-out [11].A well-differentiated angiosarcoma may resemble a benign haemangioma on MRI, because it enhances intensely on MRI with very similar patterns [1,12]. Lesion size is important to make a distinction, because angiosarcomas are rarely smaller than 2 cm and tend to arise deeper in the mammary gland; if suspicious features are seen on imaging, complete surgical excision is the treatment of choice.A cavernous haemangioma that arises in the axillary region should be finally included in the differential diagnosis of a superficial mass, such as a lymphadenopathy secondary to any unknown cancer or related to emathologic benign and malignant conditions [13].

Case report

A 40-years-old woman without familiar history of breast cancer presented in 2018 to our Center with a small smooth palpable mass of the left breast, located in the peripheric axillary estension. The mammography did not show suspicious opacities in the left breast or axilla, while the ultrasound (US) reaveled a hypoechoic, regular limited, solid mass of 18 × 11 mm in diameter (Figs. 1 and 2), with intralesional feeding vessels at Color Doppler module. There were not enlarged lymph nodes in the left axilla at the ultrasound examination, and the imaging of the right breast was negative. The finding in the left breast was classified under Breast Imaging Reporting and Data System (BI-RADS, fifth editon) assessment category 4 A (abnormality with low suspicion for malignancy). An ultrasound-guided core biopsy was performed in order to make diagnosis, beacuse the nodule was suspected to be a mammary lymphadenopathy in the context of a possible “CUP” syndrome (carcinoma unknown primary). The hystopathologic examination revealed “vascular proliferation without atypia”. The patient so underwent MR imaging which demonstrated the presence of a small round “mass-type” lesion of about 15 mm in diameter in the left axilla, isointense to the adipose tissue in the T2w sequence and with homogeneous and persistent enhancement in the T1w dynamic contrast enhanced sequences. The MRI finding was given BI-RADS category 4 A (moderate suspicion for malignancy, >10%-50%), because of the enhancement time-intensity kinetic curve (type I: slow and progressive contrast enhancement with gradual uptake of gadolinium) (Fig. 3, Fig. 4–5).In consideration of the histological data, a multidisciplinary team composed of breast surgeon, breast radiologist and oncologist decided to practice short-term imaging follow up of the lesion with mammography and ultrasound. The follow-up showed a slow tendency of the lesion to grow over time, so that in 2020 it was decided to perform a new core biopsy, with the same result of “haemangioma without atypia”. The patient presented to us at the end of 2022 for a new breast examination; mammography was still negative, but at US the lesion measured 47 × 24 mm versus 18 × 11 mm in 2018. The ultrasonographic appearence had not changed over time, and still the nodule appeared as a regular limit hypoechoic mass, with small intra-lesional feeding vessels at the Color Doppler module. The patient was so referred to the breast surgeon in order to practice excision, but before that, it was proposed to the patient an innovative study of the lesion consisting of Contrast Enhanced Ultra-Sound using Sonovue ® (BRACCO); prior to performing CEUS, an informed consent was obtained from the patient in order to exclude allergies and cardiovascular disease such as hypertension and arrhythmias.

Discussion

The CEUS (Contrast Enhanced Ultra-Sound) is a study technique that uses the ultrasound examination of a body area using a specific software already incorporated into the machine. This type of investigation takes place after intravenous administration of a specially developed contrast agent for diagnostic use (sulfur hexafluoride) and has been codified since 2004 by the European Federation of Societies for Ultrasound in Medicine and Biology (EFSUMB) [14]. The basic rationale of the clinical use of ultrasound contrast medium is to increase the echogenicity of the blood, improving the diagnostic performance of ultrasound. With specific reference to Sonovue ® (BRACCO), its use is specified in the Summary of Product Characteristics (SmPC), and includes, among others, the Color Doppler ultrasound examination in adult patients for the visualization of the vascularization of liver and breast lesions to allow a more specific characterization of the lesion [15]. In our case study, the lesion at a basal US study showed the presence of multiple internal small feeding vessels at the Color Doppler module (Fig. 6, yellow pointer).After receiving informed consent from the patient, we proceeded to inject Sonovue ® using a venous vascular access in the forearm. The contrast medium was administered in a quantity of 2.5 cc during a single diagnostic test; during the exam, the patient was assisted by the radiologist, by a resident and by a nurse. Regarding the side effects of Sonovue ®, as specified in the SmPC, its safety was evaluated in 4653 patients during 58 clinical studies. After more than 16 years of marketing, serious hypersensitivity/pseudo-allergic reactions are very rare, with an incidence rate of 1/10,000 patients exposed to the product. In clinical trials, the most frequently reported adverse reactions were headache, injection site reaction and nausea.After about 15 seconds from the administration, we assisted to the initial “wash in” of the contrast medium from the periphery to the center of the lesion, which was progressive during time (Fig. 7, yellow pointer).After about 1 minute from the administration, the lesion appeared completely bright at the CEUS, because it was full of contrast medium (Fig. 8, yellow pointer).After about 2 minutes from administration, the lesion lost the contrast medium and we assisted to a “wash out” from the center to the periphery, which was more accentuated after 3 minutes and a half (Fig. 9, yellow pointer).Breast haemangiomas are quite rare mesenchymal tumors and have variable imaging features [[9], [10], [11]]. They may have non-specific features on mammograms. Some haemangiomas may show as an opacity with well-defined margins, with or without calcifications. On US examination, a haemangioma might be seen as an oval solid mass, and the echotexture can be hypoechoic, isoechoic or heterogeneous. MRI demonstrates a variable appearance, depending on the size and subtype of the haemangioma. Dynamic contrast-enhanced MRI is necessary for accurate determination of the size and vascularity of the lesion; haemangiomas have usually an early and diffuse enhancement pattern and can mimick breast cancers. In some cases, especially when the lesion is small, US and mammography cannot identify the lesion. MRI is a more sensitive modality because of its capacity to give an assessment of vascularity features.In our case study experience, CEUS examination has demonstrated to be useful in the study of kinetics of enhancement in benign mesenchymal vascular lesions like haemangiomas, in aid of MRI. We summarize the behavior of benign breast haemangiomas in CEUS in Table 1. CEUS, in aid of MRI, provides fast information about vascularization of vascular lesion, with a good sensibility in the detection of “wash in” and “wash out” of contrast medium; in addition, it is extremely cheaper than MRI. It can be considered of choice in the follow-up imaging of haemangiomas, after a core biopsy diagnosis. CEUS on the other hand does not appear to be a useful, safe and then reasonable imaging method for the differential diagnosis of frequent non vascular lesions like fibroadenomas and typical malignant breast carcinomas (such as ductal and lobular carcinoma), which are not composed principally of vassels.

Conclusions

We hope that finally CEUS can enter in clinical practice in the study of histologically proven benign vascular lesions, in aid of MRI, especially in the follow-up imaging after a core biopsy diagnosis, this considering the substantial lack of controindications for the health of the patients, the fast execution times and the low cost for the hospital.

Patient consent

This is to state that the Patient gives full permission for the publication, reproduction, broadcast and other use of photographs, recordings and other audio-visual material and textual material (case histories) in all editions of the above-named product and in any other publication (including books, journals, CD-ROMs, online and internet), as well as in any advertising or promotional material for such product or publications. The Patient declares, in consequence of granting this permission, that has no claim on ground of breach of confidence or any other ground in any legal system against — (author's/developer's name) - and its agents, publishers, successors and assigns, in respect of such use of the photograph(s) and textual material (case histories).

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 40-year-old woman.
- Chief Complaint: Palpable lump in the left breast, slowly increasing in size over time.
- History of Present Illness: The mass was first identified in 2018 and measured 18 × 11 mm on ultrasound as a hypoechoic, well-defined solid lesion with intralesional feeding vessels. It gradually increased in size, reaching 47 × 24 mm by 2022.
- Past Medical History: None provided.
- Personal History: None provided.
- Family History: No familial history of breast cancer.
- Physical Examination: Palpable, smooth mass in the periphery of the left breast (axillary extension). No significant findings in the right breast or left axilla.
- Ancillary Tests:
  - Mammography (2018): No suspicious findings.
  - Ultrasound (2018): Hypoechoic, regular, and solid lesion with intralesional feeding vessels; categorized as BI-RADS 4A (low suspicion for malignancy).
  - Core biopsy (2018): "Vascular proliferation without atypia."
  - MRI (2018): Small mass (15 mm) isointense to adipose tissue with homogeneous and persistent enhancement. BI-RADS 4A classification due to suspicious enhancement kinetics.
  - Follow-ups: Imaging until 2020 showed gradual growth without change in morphological features. Repeat biopsy confirmed the diagnosis of "haemangioma without atypia."
  - CEUS performed in late 2022 demonstrated vascular kinetics typical of a benign vascular lesion (wash-in and wash-out phases).
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- **Benign Breast Hemangioma:** This was strongly considered due to histopathological findings in two core biopsies, imaging features, and clinical behavior (slow growth and lack of atypia). CEUS vascularity patterns further supported benignity.
- **Angiosarcoma:** Ruled out due to the lesion being smaller than 2 cm initially, its superficial location, and lack of atypia on histopathology. Although angiosarcomas can resemble hemangiomas on imaging, no evidence of malignancy was observed.
- **Fibroadenoma:** Initially considered due to the lesion's small size and benign imaging appearance, but excluded due to histological findings of vascular proliferation.
- **Complex Breast Cyst:** Excluded based on solid characteristics and feeding vessels on ultrasound.
- **Other Mesenchymal Tumors (e.g., Angiolipoma, Lymphangioma):** Excluded based on specific histological findings consistent with hemangioma.

The differentiation relied heavily on imaging, core biopsy, and patient follow-up over several years.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: The patient was diagnosed with a **benign breast hemangioma without atypia**, confirmed by histological evidence and further supported by consistent imaging findings, including CEUS and MRI.

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> Analyze the chief complaint and history of present illness. The patient is a 40-year-old woman presenting with a palpable lump in her left breast that has been slowly growing since 2018.  The initial size and subsequent growth are documented with ultrasound measurements. The lesion was described as hypoechoic, well-defined, and solid with intralesional feeding vessels on initial ultrasound.  The slow growth over several years is noted.

<step 2> Review the ancillary tests performed over time.  Mammography in 2018 was unremarkable.  Initial ultrasound and MRI in 2018 both categorized the lesion as BI-RADS 4A, indicating low suspicion for malignancy, but warranting biopsy.  The core biopsy in 2018 showed "vascular proliferation without atypia," suggesting a benign vascular lesion.  Follow-up imaging until 2020 confirmed gradual growth without morphological changes, and a repeat biopsy confirmed "haemangioma without atypia."  The CEUS in late 2022 demonstrated vascular kinetics (wash-in and wash-out) typical of a benign vascular lesion.

<step 3> Integrate all findings. The consistent description of a solid, well-defined lesion with vascular features, along with the histological confirmation of "vascular proliferation without atypia" and "haemangioma without atypia" from two separate biopsies, strongly points towards a benign vascular lesion.  The CEUS findings further support this diagnosis by demonstrating benign vascular kinetics.  Although the lesion initially received a BI-RADS 4A classification due to growth and enhancement kinetics on MRI, the repeated benign histological findings and CEUS results are more definitive. The slow, gradual growth over several years, while initially raising some concern, is not inconsistent with a benign, slowly growing vascular lesion like a haemangioma.

<step 4> Consider and exclude other possibilities. While a growing breast mass always necessitates careful evaluation to rule out malignancy, the repeated benign biopsies, consistent imaging features, and CEUS findings make a malignant diagnosis highly unlikely in this case.  The lack of suspicious features on mammography and the benign vascular characteristics on ultrasound and CEUS further diminish the likelihood of malignancy.  Fibroadenoma, another common benign breast lesion, is less likely given the vascular nature described in the reports and confirmed by histology.  Cystic lesions are excluded by the description of a solid lesion.

<step 5> Formulate the final diagnosis. Based on the comprehensive evaluation including history, physical examination, imaging, and repeated biopsies, the most likely diagnosis is a benign vascular lesion of the breast, specifically a haemangioma.  The evidence strongly supports the benign nature of the lesion.

### Answer:
Benign vascular lesion of the left breast, consistent with haemangioma.
